coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum initiators enabled direct laser writing: A technique for fabrication of dielectric, all-carbon chiral metasurfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, Arun
Rani, Sweta
Singh, Gaurav Pratap
Archana, T.
Hassan, Mahbub
Nasrin, Aklima
Gomes, Vincent G.
Saxena, Sumit
Shukla, Shobha
coredata.dc:description: Chiral metasurfaces exhibit extraordinary capabilities of steering polarization of electromagnetic waves; and hence offering enormous potential in development of ultrathin components for advanced photonic technologies such as quantum computing and communication, imaging, optical memories and logics. These structures are realized by periodically arranged subwavelength resolved artificial elements (metaelements). Metaelements are mainly metallic, dielectric or metal-dielectric based and their fabrication is a daunting task due to the associated complex fabrication methodologies involving serial, time-consuming processes and sophisticated instrumentation, hence limiting the growth, and application of these metasurfaces to its true potential. To mitigate these issues, we exploited the single-step fabrication capabilities of two-photon polymerization based lithography to generate a range of all-carbon-based dielectric chiral metasurfaces in a carbon-polymer nanocomposite resin. Additionally, theoretical responses of these structures are also calculated. These structures derive their responses from structural chirality. With the advantages of single-step fabrication of any arbitrary structure with sub-wavelength resolved features, along with the capability of rapid prototyping in the proposed material system; this work holds a great potential towards generation of all-carbon metamaterials and 3D metasurfaces for several advanced photonic and optical technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-photon active carbon dots
Chiral metasurfaces
All carbon microstructure
Two-photon lithography
Additive manufacturing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formulation of acrylate and carbon dot composite resin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of SR and ACCR; inset shows optical images of transparent SR and the brownish tint in the ACCR is observed due to presence of CDs, (b). Emission spectra of SR and ACCR at an excitation of 400 nm, revealing the emissive behaviour of ACCR due to presence of CDs; inset shows ACCR under femtosecond laser excitation. The green emission from ACCR clearly indicates the non-linear activity of the resin, (c). SEM image of suspended wire structures fabricated to check the effect of speed on fabrication resolution, (d) & (e). Zoomed view of suspended wire structures and (f). Plot of variation of lateral resolution with varying scan speeds; inset shows finest features of ∼235 nm, written at 100 mW average laser exposure. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical microscope images of(a). Modified L-type, (b). P-type, (c). G type chiral structures and SEM images of (d). C type and (e). Modified gammadion fabricated in ACCR20. Scale bars in a-c are 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of the fabricated structure and inset shows a zoomed view of single element, (b)Optical image of an array of L-shaped chiral metastructures fabricated in ACCR20, (c) Fluorescent image of the metastructures array shown in (b), (d). Computational model of showing a unit cell of the L-shaped chiral metastructures and (e). Computational model showing the presence of Eyfield component in the transmitted field, calculated at 100 THz Scale bars in (a–c) are 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The plot of transmission and reflection coefficients for RCP, LCP and frequency dependence of CD, in the range 50 THz to 110 THz for (a–c). L-shaped, (d–f). G-shaped, (g–i). C-shaped and (j–l) Modified gammadion structures, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum initiators enabled direct laser writing: A technique for fabrication of dielectric, all-carbon chiral metasurfaces

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral metasurfaces exhibit extraordinary capabilities of steering polarization of electromagnetic waves; and hence offering enormous potential in development of ultrathin components for advanced photonic technologies such as quantum computing and communication, imaging, optical memories and logics. These structures are realized by periodically arranged subwavelength resolved artificial elements (metaelements). Metaelements are mainly metallic, dielectric or metal-dielectric based and their fabrication is a daunting task due to the associated complex fabrication methodologies involving serial, time-consuming processes and sophisticated instrumentation, hence limiting the growth, and application of these metasurfaces to its true potential. To mitigate these issues, we exploited the single-step fabrication capabilities of two-photon polymerization based lithography to generate a range of all-carbon-based dielectric chiral metasurfaces in a carbon-polymer nanocomposite resin. Additionally, theoretical responses of these structures are also calculated. These structures derive their responses from structural chirality. With the advantages of single-step fabrication of any arbitrary structure with sub-wavelength resolved features, along with the capability of rapid prototyping in the proposed material system; this work holds a great potential towards generation of all-carbon metamaterials and 3D metasurfaces for several advanced photonic and optical technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Periodic spatial arrangement of array of sub-wavelength metaelements constitute metamaterials. They interact strongly with the amplitude, phase and/or polarization of electromagnetic waves and are implemented in its control/manipulation. Polarization is of special interest in development of applications such as wavefront shaping [1], wavefront modulation [2], optical amplification [3], polarimetric measurements [4], quantum communication [5], quantum computing [6], polarized light detection [7] etc. Polarization manipulation is exhibited by chiral structures due tothe lack of mirror/inversion symmetry, i.e. mirror image generated by any kind of translation and/or rotation cannot be superimposed with their original form [8]. These chiral structures enable optical activities called chiro-optic effects. For instance, rotation of plane of polarization and circular dichroism. A range of such optical activites are observed in naturally existing materials in microscopic molecular levels [9] to macroscopic crystal domain [10] and chiral surfaces [11]. These effects are observed when the EM wave travels through the chiral media. Conventionally, chiro-optic effects are attained via the use of multiple optical components such as waveplates, polarizers and mechanically rotating components; resulting in bulky systems limiting their application in miniaturized devices. For realization of chiro-optic effects at much smaller scale, incorporation of chiral dyes, chiral semiconductor transistors, liquid crystals etc., have been explored to an extent. However, these devices are prone to ageing effects and have a large response time. For realization of rapidly responding, robust and ultracompact devices exhibiting chiro-optic effects nanofabrication tools are much essential. Using these tools, a wide range of geometries, including Z-shaped antennas [12], twisted Z-shaped [13], gammadions [14], conjugated gammodians [15], helical [16–18], spiral [19–21], and L-shaped [22,23] nanostructures have been implemented towards the generation of 2D and 3D chiral metamaterials. However, their fabrication is a daunting task as it requires stringent control over dimensions and architectural attributes [24], and controlled chemical reactions [25]. Several symmetric/asymmetric chiral 3D metasurfaces including stress-induced fractal structures [26], bended metastructures [27], bidirectional origami inspired structures [28], intensity tunable metahologram [29], spectrally sensitive multiband absorbers [30] etc., have been developed using the conventional nanofabrication tools such as electron/focused-ion beam lithography, electron beam deposition etc., but these processes are serial, time-consuming and are performed under harsh experimental conditions such as high pressure/vacuum [31]. Furthermore, scalability and low throughput of these tools are problems, still to be addressed, and merging nanofabrication technologies such as self-assembly, particle synthesis, three-dimensional plasmonic nanostructure printing, glanced angled vapor deposition, charged aerosol jets based printing,femtoliters-scale meniscus based nanoparticle assembly, peptide-assisted bottom-up synthesis, and overplayed lithography, are dependent on multi-step processing for realization of the metasurfaces [32–38]. On the other hand, much simpler approaches such as arranging porous glassy carbon spheres into ordered monolayer [39], selective laser treatment of diamond [40] and femtosecond laser assisted fabrication of laser scribed graphene [41], lack either in terms of resolution (providing the minimal feature sizes on larger scales of μm to mm), or the final structures are constrained to two-dimensional patterns. To overcome these issues, two-photon polymerization (TPP) based nanofabrication can be a potential candidate for fabrication of metasurfaces; due to the inherent advantage of single-exposure step processing [42,43], comparatively high throughput and scalability [43–46], applicability to a wide range of materials [47,48] and, its capability to generate any arbitrary nanostructure with sub-wavelength resolved features in pure polymeric as well as doped resins [49–56]. TPP has been explored towards generation of plasmonic and photonic metamaterials [57–59], but the fabrication of chiral metasurfaces has not been explored much. For instance, Shukla et al., have demonstrated two-photon lithography (TPL) of y-shaped plasmonic chiral structures in gold-doped resin [60], but plasmonic metasurfaces have inherent disadvantage of metallic absorption associated losses. To counter this, Furlani et al., have implemented chiral polymer based TPP resin system to generate L-shaped chiral metasurfaces exploiting the molecular and structural chirality simultaneously [61]. However the metaelements exhibited low quality in terms of replication fidelity. In our recent work, we have demonstrated rapid prototyping of dielectric nanostructures with good replication fidelity and reproducibility [62–64], that can be extended towards realization of chiral metasurfaces exploiting the geometrical chirality of the metaelements.

We propose, acrylate and carbon dots composite resin (ACCR), for TPL of chiral metasurfaces, aiming towards the fabrication of several chiral geometric metasurfaces. We have incorporated theoretical studies to evaluate the response of these chiral geometries. Various chiral geometries including L-type, G-type and so on are fabricated with high replication fidelity and reproducibility. Furthermore we have also demonstrated fabrication over millimeter scales, revealing the scalability of the metasurfaces. All carbon-based resin, along with the achieved fabrication performance opens a new avenues for monolithic integration of these metastructures over carbon-based systems to realize next-generation ultracompact photonic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of acrylate and carbon dot composite resin
ce:section.ce:para: The details of synthesis and characterization of CDs and ACCR formulation can be found elsewhere [62,65]. Briefly, two different triacrylate monomers, ethoxylated (6) trimethylolpropane triacrylate (Sartomer SR499) and tris (2-hydroxyethyl) isocyanurate acrylate (Sartomer SR368) were mixed in equal weight (wt) proportion to obtain Sartomer resin (SR). 20 wt% of hydrothermally synthesized, powdered carbon dots (CDs) were added to the monomer mixture. The mixture was then sonicated in a bath sonicator, for 2 h to obtain homogenous solution of two-photon polymerizable resin, ACCR20. Optical properties of the SR and ACCR20 were studied using the UV–Vis NIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 2600) and spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF2400). The prepared resin formulation exhibited good stability, when stored in the dark. No noticeable change in optical properties, two-photon polymerization sensitivity or any kind of degradation was observed for the resin, for over a year.

ACCR20 was spin-coated on a glass coverslips to obtain an uniform film. The substrate was then exposed to a femtosecond laser. The details of the experimental setup for two-photon lithography is reported in our previous work [66]. Briefly, a femtosecond pulsed laser (Coherent Chameleon Ultra I; pulse width 140 fs and pulse repetition rate 80 MHz) operating at 800 nm was used as two-photon irradiation source. The beam was guided to an inverted microscope (Olympus IX81), and was subsequently focussed into ACCR20 film using a 100X, 0.9 NA objective lens. The exposed region in the focal plane exhibited two-photon polymerization, as monitored from the live feed using a high-speed EMCCD camera coupled to the microscope. To generate nano/microstructures the sample was traversed using a nanoprecision 3D translation stage. The unexposed regions were washed away using dimethyformamide to obtain the final structure. The build quality of these microstructures were examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Auriga Zeiss dual beam FEG-SEM) and confocal optical microscope (Olympus IX81) under transmission and fluorescence modes.

ACCR20 exhibits linear as well as non-linear optical properties arising from the CDs in the resin. TPL demands use of resins with low/no linear absorption at the excitation wavelength. SR is transparent as clearly seen in the inset of Fig. 1 (a); whereas ACCR has brownish appearance due to the presence of CDs in the solution. The acrylate moieties in SR, absorb deep ultraviolet wavelengths [67,68], an absorption peak at ∼230 nm in the absorption spectra of the SR shown in Fig. 1 (a); and ACCR exhibits two-additional peaks, centered on 280 nm and 340 nm. These peaks can be attributed to the π–π* transition of the carbon core and n–π* transition of the surface states of CDs in ACCR, respectively. To examine the emissive traits of the resins, photoluminescence spectrum was recorded under single-photon excitation (400 nm), SR has no emission, whereas ACCR has a strong green emission centered at 510 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 (b). For a quick investigation of the non-linear optical attributes of ACCR, femtosecond laser beam (800 nm), with 1W average power was focused using a 10X objective, the corresponding image is shown in the inset of Fig. 1 (b), where the two-photon excited fluorescence can be easily observed in ACCR.

TPL of ACCR has been extensively studied in the terms of effect of process parameters on fabrication resolution and other printing performances and a resolution of ∼245 nm was achieved at an exposure of 25 mW and a scan speed of 25 μm/s62. However, the effect of scan speed on the build resolution was not characterized systematically. We have written an array of suspended wire structures, at a fixed average power of 100 mW, and speeds were varied from 25 μm/s to 1000 μm/s. The suspended wires were supported by a cuboid on both the sides, a SEM image in Fig. 1 (c) shows a top-view of the suspended wire structure; and the zoomed views are shown in Fig. 1 (d,e). The lateral resolutions of the lines were reduced with a reduction in scan speed, as shown in Fig. 1 (f). This trend can be attributed to the increase of photon flux available to the resin as the speed is reduced; which in turn increases the amount of photogenerated radicals and hence overall polymerization volume. The finest feature of ∼235 nm was obtained at a scan speed of 800 μm/s. A zoomed-in view of the suspended wire is shown in the inset of Fig. 1 (f). Systematic investigation of the effect of CD loading in the resin and the process parameters were carried out (Figs. S1–S3), and it was observed that the tunability of lateral resolution can be easily achieved by varying resin composition and process parameters.

For the structures fabricated in ACCR, the CDs are uniformly distributed within the polymerized units, and hence they essentially get arranged in the geometry of the metaelements itself. The response of the CDs arranged in chiral geometry, and the overall response of the composite can provide useful insights towards the development of highly efficient all-carbon based dielectric metasurfaces. Evaluation and quantification of these interactions will be an interesting aspect to study and will be covered in future.

Nanofibers shown in Fig. 1 demonstrate planar, linear geometries, but spatially complex structures are also important in additive manufacturing. We have fabricated a microchess structure in ACCR, over a footprint of 240 μm
×
240 μm as shown in Fig. S4 (a). Each square is fabricated over an area of 30 μm
×
30 μm; white squares are represented by an empty area, whereas black squares are represented by a cuboidal block of 30 μm
×
30 μm
×
5 μm. The fine details are shown in Fig. S4 (b-c). Each chess character is fabricated within a volume of 30 μm
×
30 μm
×
3 μm. An image of pawn and its zoomed view, showing the intricate details are shown in Fig. S4 (d) and Fig. S4 (e-f), respectively. To demonstrate the capability of additive manufacturing of 3D structures with subwavelength resolved features, we have fabricated array of solid cones supported over a cuboid. A 3D model and a SEM image of the structure is shown in Fig. S5 (a) and S5 (b), respectively. The fabricated microstructure exhibits good co-realtion with the 3D model and, with an accuracy of ∼
250
±
2.2
nm (tip diameter of three cones were measured). The emissive traits of these microstructures were exploited towards non-invasive indepth imaging of the fabricated structures using a 405 nm excitation source, in a confocal laser scanning microscope. The images were captured at an step of 1 μm and further stacked to generate a 3D image of the microstructure at different heights. The reconstructed images of the base, cones, structure seen at a height of 20 μm and the entire structure are shown in Fig. S5 (c-f), respectively. The high quality of the reconstructed images indicates good singal to noise ratio in the fluorescent imaging. The emission intensity of the polymerized structures were found to increase with the increase on the CD loaded in the resin as shown in Fig. S6.

To demonstrate the applicability of TPL towards the generation of all-carbon-based dielectric metasurfaces, we have fabricated six different metasurfaces with chiral metaelements. These structures include L-shaped, modified L-shaped, P-shaped, G-shaped, C-shaped and gammadion geometries.

The optical micrographs and SEM images of the fabricated structures are shown in Fig. 2 (a–c) and Fig. 2 (d,e) respectively. The optical microscope image of an array of the modified-gammadion structures, and SEM image of a single element is shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, respectively. The structural attributes of L-shaped chiral metasurfaces were further examined through microscopic analysis. The uniformity of the structures over long-range order can be clearly seen in SEM, optical microscopic and fluorescent imaging shown in Fig. 3 (a, b) and (c), respectively. SEM image of a single chiral element is shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (a). CDs are uniformly distributed within the fabricated structure, as seen from the high degree of correlation in the optical microscopic and fluorescent images. Femtosecond laser laser-based fabrication of geometrically chiral structures in ACCR is useful for generation of simple as well as complex chiral metasurfaces. It is worth mentioning that the incorporation quantum dots in the chiral structures can be implemented towards the generation of metasurfaces with enhanced optical activity, as the excitonic coupling between quantum dots and polymers is known to induce increment in chiral responses [69].

Further, to evaluate the chirality of the metasurfaces, theoretical calculations were used. The electromagnetic response of the structure was modeled using full-wave electromagnetic analysis. The computational model of the L-shaped metastructure is shown in Fig. 3 (d), where it resides on a glass substrate to match the experimentally realized structure (the fabrication of the structures were carried out over a glass substrate). An electromagnetic wave with linear polarization along x-axis impinges and propagates through the metaelement, in a downward direction. The geometric chirality of the structure induces polarization change in the transmitted field, and is confirmed by the appearance of an
E y
field component in the transmitted field, as shown in Fig. 3 (e). Similar attributes were observed in the transmission profiles of all other chiral structures, as outlined in Fig. S9 (a-e); exhibiting the optical activity in all the metaelements.

Theoretical evaluation of optical activity was carried out via systematic investigation of angle of polarization and, reflection and transmission coefficients, S11 and S21, for right-handed circular polarized (RCP) and left-handed circular polarized (LCP), respectively, over frequency ranging from 50 THz to 110 THz. The plot of variation of angle of polarization for RCP and LCP are shown in Fig. S10 (a-f), respectively. Circular Dichroism (CD) is defined by the difference in magnitude of the transmission coefficients of LCP and RCP. CD is expressed as:
C D =
|
|
S
21 R
|
2
−
|
S
21 L
|
2
|
where, S21R and S21L are the reflection coefficients of RCP and LCP, respectively.

The variations in the transmission and reflection coefficients, and CD were evaluated for L-shaped, G-shaped, C-shaped, modified-gammadion, and modified L-shaped and P-shaped structures as shown in Fig. 4 (a–l) and Fig. S11 (a-f), respectively. It was observed that S11 and S21 profiles for both LCP and RCP exhibited similar traits for all the structures, exhibiting a stable response for different polarization.

The variation of CD with frequency exhibited narrow band response centered at various spectral positions depending on the architecture and the periodicity of the metastructure. It was observed that all the structures exhibited CD due to the structural chirality and, the maximum value of circular dichroism was achieved for the G-shaped metaelement. This confirms that the geometrical chirality can be explored towards the generation of all-carbon-based dielectric chiral metamaterials using TPL. We surmise that implementation of TPL of ACCR, in combination with design optimization genetic algorithm, and advanced proximity field nanopatterning techniques will be pivotal in development of new, novel metasurfaces [70,71].

We have proposed a methodology for one-step fabrication of all-carbon-based dielectric chiral metasurfaces via femtosecond laser-based writing in ACCR. Six different geometries of metasurfaces were fabricated and characterized. All the geometries exhibited structural integrity and homogeneity over long-range order. Theoretical simulations were carried out for metasurfaces consisting of L-shaped metaelements and the polarization rotation of the transmitted wave was observed for linearly polarized wave. The transmission and reflection coefficients, and CD of L-shaped, G-shaped, C-shaped and modified-gammadion shaped metaelements were also calculated, and it was observed that they exhibited considerable optical activity in THz domain. Furthermore, we have also demonstrated the fabrication of microchess structure with complex structural details and array of 3D cones with subwavelength-resolved features. With the combination of sub-wavelength resolution, and its ability to generate structures with linear as well as complex geometrical attributes, the proposed method holds the potential to induce rapid development in the field of all-carbon-based dielectric chiral metasurfaces, and is expected to instigate a paradigm shift in optical technologies.

Arun Jaiswal: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sweta Rani: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Gaurav Pratap Singh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. T. Archana: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Mahbub Hassan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Aklima Nasrin: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization. Vincent G. Gomes: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sumit Saxena: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shobha Shukla: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Co-authors MH and VG are inventors on an Australian Patent Application No. 2020901760 titled “Nanoparticle conjugates and their use in the therapeutic applications” filed on May 29, 2020.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the joint collaboration agreements between IIT Bombay and The University of Sydney. The authors extend their gratitude to Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF) and Central Facility, Industrial Research and Consultancy Centre (IRCC) of Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay (IITB) for partially providing research facilities. This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India grant via sanction order no. DST/TM/SERI/2k10/12/(G).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.03.049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spin-controlled wavefront shaping with plasmonic chiral geometric metasurfaces

Hentschel, M., Schaferling, M., Duan, X., Giessen, H. & Liu, N. Chiral plasmonics. Sci. Adv.3, e1602735 (2017).
